answer:
The following works in the corpus employ imagery of **dust** (often symbolizing humility, mortality, transience, or spiritual aspiration) or **earth** (often evoking groundedness, creation, homeland, or the material world):

**Cynewulf, *Andreas***  
This Old English poem repeatedly invokes "earth" (e.g., *fields of earth*, *middle-earth*, *earth-dwellers*, *earth-way*, *earth-grave*) in heroic-Christian contexts of divine creation, human fate, and apocalyptic judgment.[^1]

**Aṅgirasa, *Āṅgirasa Smṛti***  
"Dust" appears literally and ritually (e.g., drinking cows' dust as penance), while "earth" features in contexts of wandering, burial, and cosmic order.[^2]

**Bṛhaspati, *Bṛhaspati Smṛti***  
"Dust" in legal-ritual imagery of assault (alongside ashes and mud).[^3]

**Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1***  
Rich in both: "dust" as self-abnegation or poetic scattering (e.g., *handful of dust, I am a desert*), "earth" as homeland or cosmic plane.[^4]

**Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2***  
"Dust" evokes wandering in rational "desert-lands."[^5]

**Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3***  
"Dust" symbolizes sleeping sovereigns or sacred ground.[^6]

**Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib***  
"Dust" permeates ghazals as lover's humility or worldly nullity (e.g., *lovers' dust* free of spite).[^7]

No other works in the 37-work corpus yielded matches for these terms in English translations.

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, Ch. 1, [p-868413](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-868413).

[^2]: Aṅgirasa, *Āṅgirasa Smṛti*, Ch. 1, [p-28c82b](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-28c82b).

[^3]: Bṛhaspati, *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, Ch. 1, [p-24767c](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-24767c).

[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 5, [p-10962b](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/05-kahoon-kya-aarzoo-e-be-dili-mujh-ko-kahan-tak-hai/translation/#p-10962b).

[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, Ch. 4, [p-e0d559](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/04-yoon-to-ae-bazm-e-jahan-dilkash-thay-hungamay-tera/translation/#p-e0d559).

[^6]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 2, [p-12bbc7](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-12bbc7).

[^7]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, Ch. 1, [p-34e2c5](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/01-aa-ke-meri-jaan-ko-qarar-nahin-hai/translation/#p-34e2c5).
